---
title: "Data Foundation"
subtitle: "How I think about the storage layer"
date: 2025-01-18
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: false
---

[Engineering POV]{.section-label}

::: {.tldr}
**TL;DR** — The case studies on this site share a common storage layer: **Parquet files,
partitioned by date and source, queried with Polars and DuckDB**. This page documents the
schema choices, partitioning strategy, and query patterns. Most analytics portfolios skip
this layer entirely. I think that's a mistake — for senior analytics work, the storage
decisions *are* the work.
:::

## Why this page exists

Most portfolio sites show charts and code, but quietly assume the data fits in a CSV. That
worked in 2015. It doesn't reflect how modern analytics teams actually operate. When you're
analyzing 50GB of Wikipedia traffic or years of FEC filings, your first decision isn't
"which chart library?" — it's "how do I store this so I don't reload it from scratch every
time?"

This page documents the engineering choices behind the case studies. They're not glamorous.
They're the foundation everything else sits on.

## The decision tree

For each dataset, I work through the same sequence:

::: {.approach-grid}

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
1
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### What's the access pattern?
Is this data read once and discarded, or queried repeatedly? Are queries time-bounded
(last 30 days), entity-bounded (one client), or full-table scans? The right answer
shapes everything downstream.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
2
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### What's the natural partition key?
Almost always date or time. Sometimes a categorical (DMA, client_id). Partitioning by
the column you filter on most reduces query cost by orders of magnitude.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
3
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### What's the right format?
CSV for tiny exploratory data. Parquet for anything you'll query more than twice. JSON only when the schema is genuinely heterogeneous. Never, ever Excel as a system of record.
:::
:::

::: {.step}
::: {.step-num}
4
:::
::: {.step-body}
#### What's the right query engine?
DuckDB for ad-hoc SQL on local Parquet. Polars for transformations. Athena/BigQuery for cloud-scale. Pandas only if the data is small enough that it doesn't matter.
:::
:::

:::

## Why Parquet, specifically

Three reasons that compound:

**Columnar storage means column-selective queries are cheap.** When you read a CSV with 50
columns and only need 3, you're still parsing all 50. Parquet stores each column
contiguously, so reading 3 columns is genuinely 6% of the work, not 100%.

**Compression is dramatic and free.** Real numbers from the case studies on this site:

| Dataset | CSV size | Parquet size | Ratio |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Wikipedia hourly pageviews (1 month) | 6.2 GB | 412 MB | 15× smaller |
| FEC operating expenditures (2024 cycle) | 1.4 GB | 96 MB | 14× smaller |
| CMS Open Payments (general payments, 1 yr) | 2.1 GB | 178 MB | 12× smaller |

That's not just disk — it's network transfer time, S3 storage cost, and memory pressure
during reads. All compounding.

**Schema is part of the file.** No more "wait, is `date` a string or a timestamp?"
The Parquet file knows. Pandas, Polars, DuckDB, Athena, Spark — they all read the same file
the same way. No parsing surprises.

The cost: Parquet is binary. You can't `cat` it or open it in a text editor. That's
a fair trade for everything you get back.

## Partitioning strategy

This is where most teams get it wrong. The instinct is to partition by everything possible
("partition by year, month, day, hour, dma, channel..."). The reality: **over-partitioning
is worse than under-partitioning** because the metadata overhead destroys query performance.

The rule I use:

::: {.pullquote}
Partition by what you filter on. Don't partition by what you only group by.

[The single most useful partitioning heuristic.]{.pullquote-attr}
:::

For time-series data: partition by date. That's it. Most queries are "last N days" or "this
month" — date partitioning makes those skip entire directories of files. Adding hour-level
partitioning means thousands of tiny files, each with overhead. Not worth it unless you
genuinely run hour-bounded queries.

For mixed dimensions: partition by the *high-cardinality column you filter on*. For ad-tech
data with `client_id`, partition by client. For multi-tenant data with `region`, partition
by region. Combine with date if the volume justifies it.

The directory structure ends up looking like:

```
data/
├── wikipedia_pageviews/
│   ├── date=2024-01-01/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   ├── date=2024-01-02/
│   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   └── ...
├── fec_expenditures/
│   ├── cycle=2022/
│   │   ├── month=01/
│   │   │   └── part-0.parquet
│   │   └── month=02/...
│   └── cycle=2024/...
└── cms_payments/
    ├── year=2023/
    │   └── part-0.parquet
    └── year=2024/
        └── part-0.parquet
```

This is **Hive-style partitioning** — the directory names encode the partition values.
Every modern engine (DuckDB, Polars, Athena, Spark) auto-detects this layout.

## The query layer

For ad-hoc analysis: **DuckDB on Parquet** is the most underrated combination in modern
analytics. You get full SQL, partition pruning, column pruning, and zero infrastructure.
It runs on a laptop and queries 50GB of Parquet faster than most cloud warehouses query a
table that size.

```python
import duckdb

# Query directly against Parquet — no loading step
result = duckdb.sql("""
    SELECT
        date,
        SUM(views) AS total_views,
        COUNT(DISTINCT article) AS articles
    FROM 'data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet'
    WHERE date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-31'
      AND project = 'en.wikipedia'
    GROUP BY date
    ORDER BY date
""").df()
```

The `WHERE date BETWEEN ...` clause causes DuckDB to skip every partition outside that
range — it never even opens those files. Same query against the equivalent CSV would
take 50–100× longer.

For transformations: **Polars**. Same lazy-evaluation model as Spark, single-machine
execution, written in Rust, ~5–10× faster than pandas on real workloads. The API is more
disciplined than pandas (which I view as a feature, not a bug).

```python
import polars as pl

df = (
    pl.scan_parquet('data/wikipedia_pageviews/*/*.parquet')
    .filter(pl.col('project') == 'en.wikipedia')
    .filter(pl.col('date').is_between('2024-01-01', '2024-01-31'))
    .group_by('date')
    .agg([
        pl.col('views').sum().alias('total_views'),
        pl.col('article').n_unique().alias('articles'),
    ])
    .sort('date')
    .collect()
)
```

`scan_parquet` is the lazy version — Polars builds an execution plan, applies predicate
pushdown to the Parquet files (so only matching row-groups are read), and only materializes
the result when `.collect()` is called.

## What this signals to a hiring manager

I'm direct about why this page exists. For a Manager or Director-level analytics role,
the question isn't "can you write a SQL query?" It's "do you make decisions like an engineer
when the situation calls for it?"

Showing the storage layer signals three things:

- **I think in production terms.** Cost, latency, and reproducibility matter, not just
  the final chart.
- **I make tool choices deliberately.** Polars, DuckDB, Parquet aren't on this page because
  they're trendy. They're here because they're the right choice for the access patterns I
  described.
- **I can lead a team that operates at this level.** A team that ships code-driven reports
  needs an architecture this team can stand on. That architecture is an analytics
  leadership responsibility, not a "let the data engineering team handle it" handoff.

## Honest trade-offs

I want to be clear about what this approach *isn't* good for:

- **Real-time / streaming.** Parquet is batch. If you need sub-minute freshness, this is the wrong layer. (You'd add Kafka + a streaming engine on top.)
- **Frequent row-level updates.** Parquet files are immutable; updating individual rows
  means rewriting partitions. For mostly-append-only data (which describes most analytics
  workloads), this is fine.
- **Tiny datasets.** If your data fits comfortably in a CSV that opens in Excel, just use a CSV. The infrastructure overhead isn't justified.
- **Cross-org data sharing.** Parquet works in any modern engine, but if your stakeholders
  open everything in Excel, you'll still need to export to xlsx for delivery. (Which is
  fine — generate it from the Parquet source.)

The point isn't that Parquet/Polars/DuckDB is universally correct. The point is that
**making the choice deliberately**, and being able to explain why, is what separates senior
analytics work from junior analytics work.
